sections.body: Generative modelling with latent variables aims to model a data distribution \( p(x) \) through an auxiliary unobserved variable \( z \), with the joint \( p_\theta(x, z) = p_\theta(x \mid z)\, p(z) \) and a simple prior \( p(z) \) (typically an isotropic Gaussian \( \mathcal{N}(0, I) \)). Maximum-likelihood training requires the marginal over \( z \):

Two quantities are intractable for neural-network decoders. The marginal \( p_\theta(x) \) is an integral over a high-dimensional continuous \( z \); no closed form exists when \( p_\theta(x \mid z) \) is a neural net. The posterior \( p_\theta(z \mid x) = p_\theta(x \mid z)\, p(z) / p_\theta(x) \) inherits the same intractability via its denominator. Classical EM works when the posterior is tractable (Gaussian mixtures, factor analysis); it does not here.

sections.title: Why not a standard autoencoder?
sections.body: Common misconception: a standard autoencoder already maps \( x \) to a bottleneck code and back, so why bother with a probabilistic formulation. A deterministic autoencoder yields an arbitrary, non-smooth code space with no probabilistic interpretation: sampling \( z \sim p(z) \) and decoding does not produce realistic data because the decoder was never asked to cover a known prior. The VAE adds two couplings — a probabilistic recognition network and a KL penalty against the prior — precisely so that the decoder maps the whole prior to plausible data.

The paper introduces a practical stochastic gradient estimator for this setup: an amortized recognition network, a tight lower bound on \( \log p_\theta(x) \) called the ELBO, and a reparameterization of the sampling step so gradients flow through a differentiable path. Together these let any differentiable encoder/decoder pair be trained end-to-end with SGD on a single scalar objective.

The generative story is fixed: sample a latent \( z \sim p(z) = \mathcal{N}(0, I) \), then sample an observation \( x \sim p_\theta(x \mid z) \) from a neural-network decoder. Training requires a surrogate for the posterior \( p_\theta(z \mid x) \) used during learning — one that is tractable, trainable, and close enough to the truth to give useful gradients.

sections.title: Amortized inference
sections.body: Generative path p_θ(x | z) (solid) and recognition path q_φ(z | x) (dashed). Variational parameters φ are shared across every x.

sections.title: Amortization
sections.body: Classical variational inference optimises a separate distribution \( q(z) \) per datapoint, which costs one optimisation run per example and does not scale. The VAE introduces a recognition network \( q_\phi(z \mid x) \) whose parameters \( \phi \) are shared across all \( x \): a single forward pass predicts the approximate posterior for any input. This is amortized inference — the per-example inference cost is amortised into the shared encoder parameters. The same trick also generalises to unseen \( x \) at test time, which classical per-datapoint VI cannot.

sections.title: Gaussian parameterization
sections.body:
The paper picks a diagonal-Gaussian recognition distribution:

\[ q_\phi(z \mid x) = \mathcal{N}\bigl(z;\, \mu_\phi(x),\, \operatorname{diag}(\sigma_\phi^2(x))\bigr) \]

where \( \mu_\phi \) and \( \log \sigma_\phi^2 \) are the two outputs of the encoder MLP. The choice is not mandatory — any reparameterisable family works — but diagonal Gaussian keeps the encoder cheap, the KL closed-form (page 3), and the sampler trivially reparameterisable (page 4).
The ELBO falls out of a single identity. For any \( q_\phi(z \mid x) \), adding and subtracting \( \log q_\phi(z \mid x) \) inside the expectation and collecting terms yields:

sections.title: Log-evidence decomposition
sections.equation: \log p_\theta(x) = \mathcal{L}(\theta, \phi; x) + D_{\mathrm{KL}}\bigl(q_\phi(z \mid x)\, \|\, p_\theta(z \mid x)\bigr)

sections.body: The second term is the KL divergence between the approximate and true posterior — non-negative by construction. Therefore \( \mathcal{L}(\theta, \phi; x) \le \log p_\theta(x) \): the first term lower-bounds the log-evidence, hence "evidence lower bound" (ELBO). Maximising the ELBO in \( \theta \) pushes \( \log p_\theta(x) \) up; maximising in \( \phi \) pushes \( q_\phi(z \mid x) \) towards the true posterior.

sections.title: The ELBO — reconstruction / regularisation split
sections.equation: \mathcal{L}(\theta, \phi; x) = \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z \mid x)}\bigl[\log p_\theta(x \mid z)\bigr] - D_{\mathrm{KL}}\bigl(q_\phi(z \mid x)\, \|\, p(z)\bigr)

sections.body: The two terms have distinct roles. The first is a reconstruction term: under \( z \) sampled from the recognition network, the decoder should assign high likelihood to the observed \( x \). The second is a regularisation term: \( q_\phi(z \mid x) \) should not drift far from the prior \( p(z) = \mathcal{N}(0, I) \), so that latent codes remain globally coherent and samples \( z \sim p(z) \) decode to plausible \( x \).

sections.title: Closed-form KL for diagonal Gaussians
sections.body:
With \( q_\phi(z \mid x) = \mathcal{N}(\mu, \operatorname{diag}(\sigma^2)) \) and \( p(z) = \mathcal{N}(0, I) \), the KL has an analytic form (Appendix B of the paper):

\[ D_{\mathrm{KL}} = -\tfrac{1}{2} \sum_{j=1}^{d_z} \bigl(1 + \log \sigma_j^2 - \mu_j^2 - \sigma_j^2\bigr) \]

Only the reconstruction term still needs Monte-Carlo estimation; the KL is computed exactly in closed form. This keeps the estimator's variance low enough for L = 1 sample per batch to suffice (page 5).

Optimising the ELBO in \( \phi \) requires gradients of \( \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z \mid x)}[\log p_\theta(x \mid z)] \) with respect to \( \phi \). The naïve score-function estimator \( \nabla_\phi \mathbb{E}_q[f] = \mathbb{E}_q[f\, \nabla_\phi \log q] \) is unbiased but has variance too high for practical SGD at this scale. The reparameterization trick rewrites the sample as a deterministic function of parameters and external noise, making the gradient pathwise.

sections.title: Gradient flow under reparameterization
sections.body: Encoder outputs μ_φ(x), σ_φ(x); stochasticity lives in ε. Gradients w.r.t. φ flow through the deterministic (μ, σ) path.

sections.title: Why the rewrite helps
sections.body: With \( z = g(\epsilon, \phi, x) \), expectations under \( q_\phi \) become expectations under the fixed distribution of \( \epsilon \), and the integrand is differentiable in \( \phi \) through \( g \). The pathwise estimator \( \nabla_\phi \mathbb{E}_\epsilon[f(g(\epsilon, \phi, x))] = \mathbb{E}_\epsilon[\nabla_\phi f(g(\epsilon, \phi, x))] \) is also unbiased and has dramatically lower variance than the score-function estimator — the shared randomness \( \epsilon \) across the forward and backward pass is the reason.

sections.title: Not every distribution is reparameterizable
sections.body: Common misconception: reparameterization is a universal trick. It works cleanly for location-scale families (Gaussian, Laplace, uniform), and with more care for others (concrete / Gumbel-softmax for categoricals; Kumaraswamy for Beta). Genuinely discrete latents do not admit a pathwise gradient and require alternatives such as straight-through estimators (used by VQ-VAE) or score-function with control variates.

The paper demonstrates the estimator on modest MLPs — the method is architecture-agnostic, and the experimental choices reflect what was practical on a 2013 single-GPU machine. All numbers below are from §5 of the paper.

sections.title: Experimental setup
sections.body:
SettingValue
Encoder / decoderMLP with 500 hidden units, tanh activation
Latent dimensionality \( d_z \)swept over { 3, 5, 10, 20, 200 }
Batch size \( M \)100
MC samples \( L \) per datapoint1 (sufficient when \( M \ge 100 \))
OptimiserAdagrad
DatasetsMNIST (binary), Frey Face (continuous)

sections.title: Decoder family depends on the data
sections.body:
The decoder's output distribution matches the data type:

  • Bernoulli decoder for binary pixels (MNIST): \( p_\theta(x \mid z) = \prod_i \mathrm{Bern}(x_i;\, \pi_i(z)) \), where the decoder outputs logits fed through a sigmoid. The reconstruction term becomes binary cross-entropy.
  • Gaussian decoder for continuous pixels (Frey Face): \( p_\theta(x \mid z) = \mathcal{N}(x;\, \mu_\theta(z),\, \sigma_\theta^2(z)\, I) \). The decoder outputs mean and (log-)variance per pixel. The reconstruction term becomes squared error scaled by the predicted variance.

The rest of the pipeline — encoder, reparameterisation, KL term — is identical across the two.

sections.title: Why L = 1 works
sections.body: A batch of size \( M = 100 \) already averages 100 independent noise draws \( \epsilon^{(m)} \), one per datapoint. The gradient estimator is therefore an average over 100 pathwise samples even with one sample per datapoint — low-variance enough for stable SGD. Increasing \( L \) per datapoint was tested but did not materially improve training in the paper's experiments.

The paper's headline experiments compare AEVB (the proposed training algorithm) against the wake-sleep algorithm, the dominant prior method for training deep generative models with recognition networks. Both methods train the same MLP architecture on the same data; only the gradient estimator differs.

sections.title: Bound vs compute
sections.body: On both MNIST and Frey Face, AEVB reached higher variational lower bounds than wake-sleep and reached them faster per training sample, across every latent dimensionality tested (\( d_z \in \{3, 5, 10, 20, 200\} \)). The gap was largest for larger \( d_z \), where wake-sleep's coordinate-ascent-style update struggles with high-dimensional latent spaces.

sections.title: Marginal likelihood estimates
sections.body: For MNIST with small training sets, the paper additionally estimates the true marginal log-likelihood via importance sampling through the trained \( q_\phi \). AEVB again outperformed wake-sleep; the gap narrowed with larger training sets, consistent with both methods eventually saturating at the data's true log-likelihood.

sections.title: Qualitative samples
sections.body: The paper also shows generated samples from the prior — \( z \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I) \) fed through the trained decoder. The MNIST samples are recognisable digits; the Frey Face samples are coherent faces with smooth interpolations as \( z \) is varied along a line in latent space. The continuity is not an accident: the KL-to-prior term is what forces the decoder to map the whole Gaussian ball to plausible data, which is also why simple autoencoders (with no KL term) fail at unconditional sampling.

The original paper's MLP decoders never had enough capacity to ignore \( z \), so the training dynamics were benign. Later work combined VAEs with autoregressive decoders (PixelRNN/PixelCNN) or strong text decoders, and ran headlong into posterior collapse: the recognition network converges to the prior everywhere, the KL term goes to zero, and the decoder generates from its own prior without using the latent code.

sections.title: Why powerful decoders break the VAE
sections.body: Common misconception: a more powerful decoder always makes the VAE better. When the decoder's marginal \( \int p_\theta(x \mid z)\, p(z)\, dz \) already models the data well without \( z \), the optimiser finds a local minimum where \( q_\phi(z \mid x) = p(z) \) for all \( x \) — the regularisation term is zero, the reconstruction term is modelled by the decoder alone, and the latent code carries no information. Fixes: KL annealing (scale the KL term by \( \beta_t \) rising from 0 to 1 during training), free bits (forbid the KL from dropping below a threshold per latent dimension), and architectural restrictions on decoder capacity.

sections.title: Descendants and relatives
sections.source: Author synthesis; each line has its own paper.
sections.body:
VariantWhat it changes
β-VAE (Higgins et al., 2017)Weights the KL term by \( \beta > 1 \) to trade reconstruction for disentangled latent dimensions.
VQ-VAE (van den Oord et al., 2017)Discrete codebook latents trained with a straight-through estimator; bypasses reparameterisation.
Hierarchical / NVAEDeep stacks of latent groups \( z_1, z_2, \ldots \) with top-down generation.
Latent diffusion (Stable Diffusion)VAE compresses images to a small latent grid; a diffusion model generates in that latent space.
Normalising flowsExact likelihoods via invertible maps; trades model expressivity for a tighter bound (no bound at all — the likelihood is exact).

sections.title: Why the paper still matters
sections.body: Two specific contributions from 2014 persisted into every subsequent deep generative model: amortized inference via a shared recognition network, and the reparameterization trick for low-variance pathwise gradients through stochastic sampling. Both are used in modern diffusion training, in RL policy-gradient variants, and anywhere a differentiable surrogate is needed for an otherwise-stochastic operation. The VAE itself now most often appears as the image encoder in front of a latent diffusion model, not as the final generator.

quiz.prompt: Why are both the marginal \( p_\theta(x) \) and the posterior \( p_\theta(z \mid x) \) intractable for a neural-network decoder, and why does classical EM not rescue us?
quiz.answer: The marginal \( p_\theta(x) = \int p_\theta(x \mid z)\, p(z)\, dz \) is an integral over high-dimensional continuous \( z \) with no closed form when \( p_\theta(x \mid z) \) is a neural net. The posterior \( p_\theta(z \mid x) = p_\theta(x \mid z)\, p(z) / p_\theta(x) \) inherits the intractability via its denominator. EM requires an E-step that computes (or tractably represents) the exact posterior — feasible for Gaussian mixtures and factor analysis, not for neural decoders.

quiz.prompt: What does "amortized" mean in amortized variational inference, and why does it matter at scale?
quiz.answer: Classical variational inference fits a separate variational distribution \( q(z) \) per datapoint, paying one optimisation run for each example. Amortized inference replaces that per-example cost with a single shared recognition network \( q_\phi(z \mid x) \) whose parameters \( \phi \) are learned once across the whole dataset. Inference at test time is then a single forward pass; the network also generalises to unseen \( x \), which per-example VI cannot.

quiz.prompt: State the identity that produces the ELBO from \( \log p_\theta(x) \), and explain what maximising the ELBO does to both \( \theta \) and \( \phi \).
quiz.answer: \( \log p_\theta(x) = \mathcal{L}(\theta, \phi; x) + D_{\mathrm{KL}}(q_\phi(z \mid x)\, \|\, p_\theta(z \mid x)) \). Because the KL on the right is non-negative, \( \mathcal{L} \le \log p_\theta(x) \) — hence "lower bound". Maximising \( \mathcal{L} \) over \( \theta \) raises \( \log p_\theta(x) \) (since the KL bounds the gap). Maximising over \( \phi \) drives the KL towards zero, pushing \( q_\phi(z \mid x) \) towards the true posterior.

quiz.prompt: Why is the score-function estimator \( \nabla_\phi \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi}[f] = \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi}[f\, \nabla_\phi \log q_\phi] \) inadequate for training VAEs, and what does the reparameterization trick replace it with?
quiz.answer: The score-function estimator is unbiased but has variance that scales poorly with \( d_z \) because the signal \( f abla \log q \) correlates weakly with \( \phi \). Reparameterization writes the sample as \( z = g(\epsilon, \phi, x) = \mu_\phi(x) + \sigma_\phi(x) \odot \epsilon \) with \( \epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I) \). The pathwise estimator \( abla_\phi \mathbb{E}_\epsilon[f(g(\epsilon, \phi, x))] = \mathbb{E}_\epsilon[ abla_\phi f(g(\epsilon, \phi, x))] \) shares \( \epsilon \) between forward and backward pass, dramatically reducing variance.

quiz.prompt: With \( q_\phi(z \mid x) \) a diagonal Gaussian and \( p(z) = \mathcal{N}(0, I) \), why does the paper need only \( L = 1 \) Monte-Carlo sample per datapoint?
quiz.answer: The KL term has a closed-form solution for diagonal Gaussians (Appendix B of the paper) so no Monte-Carlo is needed there. Only the reconstruction term \( \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi}[\log p_\theta(x \mid z)] \) is estimated by sampling. A minibatch of size \( M = 100 \) averages over 100 independent \( \epsilon \) draws even with one sample per datapoint, so the gradient's variance is already small. Larger \( L \) was tested but did not materially improve training.

quiz.prompt: The paper compares AEVB against the wake-sleep algorithm. On what axes did AEVB win, and where was the gap largest?
quiz.answer: AEVB reached higher variational lower bounds than wake-sleep across every latent dimensionality tested (\( d_z \in \{3, 5, 10, 20, 200\} \)) on both MNIST and Frey Face, and it reached them with fewer training samples (Figure 2 of arXiv:1312.6114). The gap grew with \( d_z \); wake-sleep's coordinate-ascent-style update is particularly inefficient in high-dimensional latent spaces, while AEVB's joint pathwise gradient handles large \( d_z \) without penalty.

quiz.prompt: What is posterior collapse, why did the original 2014 paper not suffer from it, and what are two standard fixes?
quiz.answer: Posterior collapse is the training pathology where \( q_\phi(z \mid x) \to p(z) \) for all \( x \), the KL term goes to zero, and the decoder generates from its own prior without using \( z \). It requires a decoder strong enough to model \( p(x) \) on its own — the original paper's shallow MLP decoders could not, so the phenomenon only became prominent with later autoregressive/text decoders. Standard fixes: KL annealing (scale the KL term by \( \beta_t \) from 0 to 1 during training) and free bits (forbid the KL from falling below a per-dimension threshold).

quiz.prompt: Two contributions from the 2014 paper persisted into essentially every deep generative model that followed. What are they, and where do they show up in modern systems?
quiz.answer: (1) Amortized inference via a shared recognition network replaced per-datapoint variational fits with a single forward pass — now ubiquitous in any model with a learned posterior or encoder. (2) The reparameterization trick for pathwise gradients through sampling — used in every diffusion training loop, in stochastic policies for RL, in Gumbel-softmax for approximately-discrete choices, and in any situation where a stochastic node sits between parameters and a loss. The VAE itself most often appears today as the image encoder feeding a latent-diffusion model.
